coredata.dc:title: Nonmetal-doped quantum dot-based fluorescence sensing facilitates the monitoring of environmental contaminants

coredata.dc:description: Environmental pollution is the main threatening factor to human health, survival and global sustainable development. Achieving rapid, sensitive detection of contaminants is extremely important for timely environmental pollution monitoring and treatment. Quantum dot (QD) probes have become a common method for the detection of contaminants. In particular, the development of QDs based on nonmetallic element-doped carbon materials QDs and other nonmetallic doped Si QDs, MoOx QDs, MoS2 QDs, and MXene QDs provides a more convenient and effective means of detecting pollutants. However, a comprehensive summary of the application of nonmetal-doped QD probes for contaminant detection is still lacking. To address this issue, in the present work we mainly categorize different nonmetal-doped QDs into “top-down” and “bottom-up” strategies based on their preparation methods. QD probes based on nonmetal doping have unique optical properties, such as a narrow excitation spectrum, optical tunability, high fluorescence quantum yield (QY), and fluorescence stability. Fluorescence sensing technology can be realized through sensing mechanisms such as fluorescence/dynamic quenching, photoinduced electron transfer (PET), internal filtering effect (IFE), and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Considering the ease of implementation, operation, and immediate response of fluorescence sensing technology, it has been widely used in research for the detection of environmental pollutants. We have found that fluorescence sensing technology based on nonmetal-doped QD probes can achieve rapid detection of pollutants (such as heavy metals in water or food, harmful nonmetallic ions, organic pesticides, and antibiotic residues), and its limit of detection (LOD) can reach the picomolar level for trace detection. In addition, the fluorescence sensing technology of nonmetallic QD probes can be combined with smart devices to realize real-time monitoring of pollutants. Our work provides additional strategies for developing nonmetal-doped QD probe-based detection of contaminants and advancing future environmental governance.

head.ce:title: Nonmetal-doped quantum dot-based fluorescence sensing facilitates the monitoring of environmental contaminants

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Key Laboratory of Green Pesticide, Key Laboratory of Green Pesticide and Agricultural Bioengineering, Ministry of Education, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Environmental pollution is the main threatening factor to human health, survival and global sustainable development. Achieving rapid, sensitive detection of contaminants is extremely important for timely environmental pollution monitoring and treatment. Quantum dot (QD) probes have become a common method for the detection of contaminants. In particular, the development of QDs based on nonmetallic element-doped carbon materials QDs and other nonmetallic doped Si QDs, MoOx QDs, MoS2 QDs, and MXene QDs provides a more convenient and effective means of detecting pollutants. However, a comprehensive summary of the application of nonmetal-doped QD probes for contaminant detection is still lacking. To address this issue, in the present work we mainly categorize different nonmetal-doped QDs into “top-down” and “bottom-up” strategies based on their preparation methods. QD probes based on nonmetal doping have unique optical properties, such as a narrow excitation spectrum, optical tunability, high fluorescence quantum yield (QY), and fluorescence stability. Fluorescence sensing technology can be realized through sensing mechanisms such as fluorescence/dynamic quenching, photoinduced electron transfer (PET), internal filtering effect (IFE), and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Considering the ease of implementation, operation, and immediate response of fluorescence sensing technology, it has been widely used in research for the detection of environmental pollutants. We have found that fluorescence sensing technology based on nonmetal-doped QD probes can achieve rapid detection of pollutants (such as heavy metals in water or food, harmful nonmetallic ions, organic pesticides, and antibiotic residues), and its limit of detection (LOD) can reach the picomolar level for trace detection. In addition, the fluorescence sensing technology of nonmetallic QD probes can be combined with smart devices to realize real-time monitoring of pollutants. Our work provides additional strategies for developing nonmetal-doped QD probe-based detection of contaminants and advancing future environmental governance.

ce:keyword.ce:text: N-MQDs, chlorine, nitrogen-codoped Ti3C2 MXene quantum dots
P-MoOx QDs, nitrogen, phosphorus-codoped MoOx quantum dots
P-CQDs, nitrogen, phosphorus-codoped carbon quantum dots
S-CQDs, nitrogen, sulfur-codoped carbon quantum dots
sulfur, oxygen-codoped carbon nitride quantum dot

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution is a crucial factor affecting human health, survival, and global sustainable development. According to a recent report, two-thirds of chronic diseases and approximately 16 % of premature deaths worldwide are related to environmental pollution [1]. Environmental pollution affects the survival of non-target organisms such as bees and fish, jeopardizes plant health, and reduces crop yields by 5 %− 10 % [2,3]. Therefore, it is highly essential to detect environmental pollutants to ensure human health, survival, and environmental safety.

The development of detection technology is extremely important for the detection of contaminants. Currently, there are three main sensing methods that can achieve the qualitative and quantitative detection of contaminants, ion chromatography, mass spectrometry, and nanoprobes. For example, the presence of quaternary ammonium ions in river samples can be determined by combining solid phase extraction and ion chromatography [4]. N, N-dimethylcyclohexylamine is a typical industrial compound, and N, N-dimethylbenzylamine is a multipurpose chemical used in the manufacture of agriculture, paints, plastics, textile dyes, and pigments. The detection of these two organic pollutants in Arctic snow samples can be effectively achieved using extraction techniques and two-dimensional gas chromatography-high-resolution mass spectrometry [5]. The bimetallic PbCdSe QD gel containing atomically dispersed lead ion sites achieves a strong NO2 sensor response [6]. Recently widely reported nonmetal-doped carbon dots (CDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) based on carbon-based nanomaterials also enable contaminant detection. Such as the fluorescence of nitrogen, sulfur-codoped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) can be quenched by Hg2+ through strong electron transfer and ligand interactions, thus enabling the trace detection of Hg2+ [7]. Several detection techniques, such as ion chromatography, high-performance liquid chromatography, and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, can be used to detect pollutants. However, these technologies have certain drawbacks, such as being time-consuming, expensive, and incapable of detecting target chemicals at the sample source in situ. Compared with conventional nanofluorescent sensing materials, QD probes based on nonmetal doping have enhanced photoluminescence (PL), better photostability, biocompatibility, and higher sensitivity. It provides significant advantages in detection mode and detection efficiency, making it a widely adopted method.

In recent years, nonmetal-doped QD probe sensors have become an important tool for environmental pollutant detection with extensive applications in heavy metals, pesticides, harmful nonmetal ions, and toxic gas pollutants. For example, MXenes have recently been developed as a broad range of two-dimensional inorganic materials including transition metal carbides, nitrides, and/or carbon-nitrides. This 2D inorganic material can be prepared by solvothermal method to produce nitrogen-doped Ti3C2 MXene quantum dots (N-MXene QDs), which enables sensitive detection of Cu2+ [8]. Blue fluorescence nitrogen-doped Si quantum dots (N-Si QDs) enable precise detection of Hg2+ by fluorescence quenching and can be used for imaging biological thiols in cells [9]. Sensing arrays based on three doped QD probes, including N-doped graphene, N, S-codoped graphene, and graphene oxide, are capable of detecting pesticides such as fluoroxypyr-methyl, bensulfuron-methyl, fomesafen, and diafenthiuron [10]. In addition, a new iodine doping strategy was recently reported for the preparation of iodine-doped MoS2 quantum dots (I-MoS2 QDs) with good environmental friendliness, hydrophilicity, and photocorrosion resistance, which can realize F- detection in water [11]. Graphitic carbon nitride, as an emerging class of two-dimensional metal-free polymeric nanomaterials, has emerged as a replacement for a number of sensor devices. Boron-doped graphitic carbon nitride QDs are possible to detect NO2 at room temperature based on coated Langloisite surface acoustic wave sensors [12]. Compared to metal-doped QD probes, nonmetal-doped QD probes have lower preparation costs, higher optical stability, and good biocompatibility. Consequently, sensing fluorescence based on nonmetal-doped QDs has important potential for applications in environmental pollutant detection. Some QD probes, such as CDs and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have been discussed for applications in pollutant detection.

However, a comprehensive summary of QD probes based on nonmetal doping for contaminant detection is still lacking. In this work, recent advances in synthesizing nonmetal-doped QDs are summarized. The excellent optical properties, fluorescence sensing mechanism, as well as the applications in the detection of environmental pollutants are discussed. Finally, the challenges and prospects of nonmetal-doped QD probes for environmental pollutant detection are presented.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation methods of nonmetal-doped QD-based probes
ce:section.ce:para: The production of QD probes doped with nonmetal elements can be mainly classified as two types of strategies, “top-down” and “bottom-up” paths [13]. The “top-down” approach involves processing larger-sized precursor materials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, and nanosheets into desired nanoparticles by various chemical, physical, or electrochemical routes [14]. This approach mainly involves methods such as electrochemistry and laser ablation, which offer the advantage of preserving the structure and characteristics of the original material to some extent. The “bottom-up” approach uses small organic molecules or biological resources as precursors, such as glucose, urea, and chitosan, which are further subjected to hydrothermal reactions, pyrolysis, or combustion to acquire desirable QDs of nanomaterials [15]. This strategy mainly involves the use of hydrothermal, microwave-assisted, continuous hydrothermal flow synthesis (CHFS), with the advantages of utilizing a wide range of raw materials and being environmentally friendly ( Fig. 1). Table 1 summarizes the nonmetal-doped QD probes prepared by different strategies in recent years. In the following sections, we discuss the application of these methods in nonmetal-doped QD synthesis.

Electrochemical techniques involve applying a voltage to the precursors using electrodes to oxidize or reduce them, resulting in the production of nonmetallic element-doped QD probes with uniform sizes. It has the advantages of avoiding high-temperature reactions, preparing nonmetal-doped QDs with uniform particle size, and causing less environmental damage. This strategy has been widely used in the synthesis of chlorine, nitrogen-codoped Ti3C2 MXene quantum dots (Cl, N-MQDs), and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) [16,17]. For example, it was reported that N, Cl-MQDs were prepared using tetramethylammonium hydroxide and NH4Cl as electrolytes, bulk Ti3AlC2 as the working electrode, and platinum wire as the counter electrode. In this process, Cl- has a strong binding affinity to Al, and N-containing electrolyte ions or small molecules can promote nitrogen doping in graphene structures driven by electric potential [16] ( Fig. 2a). In addition, N-GQDs with uniform particle sizes were obtained using three-dimensional carbon nanotubes as the raw material and a two-electrode system [17]. Given the need to carefully consider the selection of doping solvents and maintain precise voltage control, this method is more demanding in terms of equipment and raw materials and typically requires professional equipment support.

The laser ablation method uses high-energy laser pulses to irradiate the surface of a target material, generating high temperature and pressure that leads to the formation of doped QD nanoparticles. This approach offers several advantages, including simplicity, eco-friendliness, and the absence of postpurification treatment. Notably, laser ablation has been extensively applied in the preparation of N- or S-doped GQDs [18,19]. For example, the preparation of N-GQDs from graphite flakes and diethylenetriamine by the pulsed laser ablation process has been reported. In a typical process, the nanosecond pulsed laser phenomenon is typically associated with the generation of a plasma plume, cavitation bubbles, and thermal decomposition near the surface of the target [18] (Fig. 2b). In addition, graphite flakes were dissolved in a mixture of 3-mercaptopropionic acid and high-purity ethanol. Ablation with a pulsed laser for a period of time, and then injected into the graphite solution with a horizontal pulsed laser beam at a repetitive frequency. This process resulted in the preparation of S-doped GQDs with high QYs [19]. A limitation of this method is the preparation of nonmetal-doped QD probes with uniform particle size, which poses a challenge in terms of large-scale production.

Hydrothermal methods use many biological sources, oxides, hydroxides, or elemental salts as precursor substances, dissolution in hot water at high temperature and pressure for several hours results in QD probes doped with different elements. The hydrothermal method has the good characteristics of easy preparation, low cost, high efficiency, and eco-friendliness and is the most widely used route for doped QD synthesis [20]. Many nonmetals are doped into the host QDs in a single form or codoped form, such as sulfur-doped Si quantum dots (S-Si QDs) and nitrogen-doped MoS2 quantum dots (N-MoS2 QDs) [21,22]. For example, trisodium citrate was dissolved in water, 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane was injected, and L-cysteine was added when the solution was completely dissolved. The precursor solution was then transferred to a stainless-steel autoclave lined with tetrafluoroethylene. Finally, blue fluorescent S-Si QDs were obtained with a QY of 66 % [21] ( Fig. 3a). Ammonium tetrathiomolybdate and cystamine dihydrochloride were dispersed in pure water as precursors, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and kept to produce N-MoS2 QDs with a QY of 14.9 % [22]. The application of the hydrothermal method is limited by the time consumption and the difficulty of obtaining nonmetal-doped QD powder directly, and the high temperature and pressure conditions are dependent on the equipment.

The microwave-assisted method uses electromagnetic radiation to heat the precursor and rapidly increase its temperature in a short time, resulting in the formation of nonmetallic element-doped nanoprobes. This method is not only economical, convenient, and fast but also has a short reaction time and does not require high temperature or pressure [23]. For example, xylan and NH4OH were used as precursors dissolved in water, and the solution was transferred to a microwave system for heating to obtain brownish-yellow nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) [24]. Urea and citric acid were used as N and B sources, and the configuration was put into a microwave oven frequency with a proportional-integral-derivative controller to adjust the heating rate, pulse frequency, and temperature. The final products obtained were N-GQDs and N, B-codoped GQDs [25] (Fig. 3b). In addition, nitrogen, phosphorus-codoped MoOx quantum dots (N, P-MoOx QDs) were prepared by a microwave-assisted method using phosphomolybdic acid hydrate (H3P (Mo3O10)4-xH2O) and glutathione as precursors [26]. It is challenging to maintain an exact temperature in the microwave method, so achieving high-performance preparation of nonmetal-doped QDs is difficult and requires precise temperature control.

CHFS is a method that involves mixing precursors with supercritical water and heating the mixture in a microfluidic reactor to produce nonmetal-doped QDs in a fast and environmentally friendly manner. The CHFS method offers several advantages over conventional methods, such as delivering high-quality nanomaterials in a cleaner and faster manner and allowing for controlled nucleation to regulate particle size [27]. This method is mainly used for the preparation of nitrogen, phosphorus-codoped carbon quantum dots (N, P-CQDs), or nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) [28,29]. For example, N, P-CQDs were prepared by mixing ethanolamine with 40% phosphoric acid using the CHFS method. The size and morphology of the N, P-CQDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy, and were found to be well dispersed and homogeneous in size [28] (Fig. 3c). A recent report described the use of a continuous hot-flow method with citric acid to produce N-CDs that emit blue light. The preparation time was reduced from hours to seconds, and the ability to adjust and regulate reaction parameters, such as temperature, pressure, and flow rate, allows for greater durability and control [29]. In CHFS, a typical challenge is enhancing the synthesis efficiency by optimizing the connectivity or compatibility between the reaction and its steps.

The optical properties of doped nonmetallic QD probes mainly include narrower emission spectra, fluorescence tunability, high fluorescence QY, and high fluorescence stability. The optical properties of QD probes are crucial for enhancing their sensitivity and stability in biosensors. For example, high-intensity fluorescence can make the probe more sensitive, and optical tunability can improve the selectivity of the probe. High stability of fluorescence can enable the probe to maintain high fluorescence intensity in different environments and thus improve its detection efficiency. The optical properties of nonmetal-doped QDs depend mainly on product purity, size, conformation, and surface defects. In the next section, we discuss how nonmetallic doping elements affect the optical properties of host QD probes.

The PL emission spectra play a crucial role in characterizing the optical properties of QD probes doped with nonmetal atoms. The emission spectra of doped nonmetallic QDs are narrower, and most exhibit excitation-dependent photoluminescence. For nonmetal-doped CQDs, the emission spectrum generally remains between 400 − 550 nm and appears as blue or green light. For example, the most common N-CQD have a strong absorption peak at 320 nm was observed in the UVvis absorption spectrum, which can be ascribed to the n-π * transition of CO groups on the surface of N-CQDs. Meanwhile, the optimal excitation wavelength (λex = 320 nm) and emission wavelength (Em = 390 nm) of N-CQDs were obtained from the emission and excitation spectra, respectively, and the excitation-dependent emission of N-CQDs was exhibited [30] ( Fig. 4a). The emission spectra of N-Si QDs are similar to those of nonmetal-doped CDs, which emit maximum fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths of 260 nm and 425 nm, respectively [31]. In addition, intense emission of N, B-codoped GQDs was observed in the near-infrared region (650 − 900 nm). Presumably due to the local distortion of electronic energy caused by the doping of N and B atoms, the presence of vacancy defects causes a shift in the emission peak toward the near-infrared window [32]. The redshifted or blueshifted features of the emission spectra are valuable for gaining insight into the emission properties of nonmetal-doped QD probes.

The optical tunability of nonmetal-doped QD probes is the main reason that the designed probes exhibit multiple fluorescences. The doping of nonmetal elements mainly affects the conformational and dimensional changes of the host QD probes, which in turn allow the probe to achieve high intensity and stable fluorescence emission at different excitation wavelengths. For example, the size of the N, P-MoOx QDs can be controlled by curating the duration of microwave radiation to obtain size-dependent emission N, P-MoOx QDs. So, N, P-MoOx QDs also show the same size-dependent emission properties as conventional QDs [33]. N-GQDs synthesized from microplasma-treated chitosan electrolytes show normalized PL emission spectra at different pH values and gradually redshift as the pH of the electrolyte increases. This is attributed to the fact that surface functional groups, energy level gaps induced by surface defects, and edge states of N-GQDs can be adjusted at different pH values, which in turn adjusts the emission properties of the N-GQDs [34] (Fig. 4b). The optical tunability of nonmetal-doped QD probes has potential applications in the development of multifunctional QD probes and biological imaging.

Fluorescence QY is one of the important fluorescent properties of nonmetal-doped QD probes, doping with nonmetal elements is an important way to increase QD fluorescence QY. For example, it has been reported that N-MQDs have an energy gap and a subenergy gap (0.15 eV) for pristine MQDs. However, due to the introduction of N atoms, the energy gap broadens and incorporates gap states in the subenergy gap. As a result, N-MQDs help to reduce the charge and energy losses and thus improve the PL QY, resulting in a QY as high as 18.7 % [35]. In addition, the fluorescence QYs of QDs doped with different elements are different. By comparing the fluorescence QYs of pristine GQDs, S-doped GQDs, N, S-codoped GQDs, and N-GQDs with quinine sulfate as a blank control, it was found that N-GQDs had the highest QYs of 78 %. This confirms that N-doping favors the fluorescence emission of GQDs more than S-doping [36] (Fig. 4c). Thus, the increase in fluorescence QY in turn enhances the emission intensity of nonmetal-doped QDs, which is important for enhancing the sensitivity of nonmetal-doped QD probes.

Resistance to photobleaching is an important indicator of the fluorescence properties of nonmetal-doped QD probes and is also a critical factor in maintaining their photostability in different environmental conditions. Doping with nonmetallic elements can affect the quantum effect of the host QD probe so that it can also maintain a high fluorescence intensity under different conditions. Nitrogen, sulfur-codoped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQDs) emit bright blue fluorescence in aqueous solution under UV irradiation at 365 nm. There was no obvious change in the fluorescence intensity after 10 h of continuous excitation, confirming the excellent anti-photo-bleaching property of the probe [37]. N-MoS2 QDs have been reported to have good photostability, and their fluorescence intensity is maintained by 95 % and 97 % when N-MoS2 QDs are placed in NaCl and H2O2, respectively [22]. Doping of nonmetallic QDs plays an important role in improving their photobleaching ability and is an important means to improve the detection efficiency of the probe.

Fluorescence lifetime is another important characteristic to measure the optical stability of fluorescent sensing materials. The doping of nonmetals can improve the efficiency of trapping the electron localization state and the hole removal efficiency of the electron donor, resulting in a significant increase in fluorescence lifetime. For example, in N-doped graphitic CDs, N doping improves the hole removal efficiency of the electron donor. As a result, this approach can effectively prolong the lifetime of photoexcited electrons, leading to highly efficient photocatalytic performance [38]. As reported, no obvious color difference was observed in the emitted fluorescence of N, Br-codoped GQDs under different pH conditions. The PL lifetime of N, Br-codoped GQDs was examined, and the average lifetime was calculated to be 7.8 ns [39]. The addition of dopants significantly improves the fluorescence lifetime and allows for a wider range of applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of nonmetal-doped QD based fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing mechanism of nonmetal-doped QD based fluorescent probes is similar to that of traditional QD probes, and they mainly involve dynamic and static quenching, photoinduced electron transfer (PET), internal filtering effect (IFE), and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [40] ( Fig. 5). The fluorescence of nonmetal-doped QD probes can be directly enhanced or quenched by the analyte based on the sensing principles, or it can be quenched after binding to a quencher (nonanalyte) and then restored upon the introduction of the analyte (turn-off-on). Consequently, nonmetal-doped QD probes with enhanced electron donor or acceptor functionality have become a crucial component of various sensors for detection. In the following section, we focus on the specific characteristics and properties of the various mechanisms of nonmetal-doped QDs.

Static and dynamic quenching sensing mechanisms are widely used for contaminant detection because of their simple and fast detection signal response. Static quenching means that the excited fluorophore directly combines with the quenching agent to form a complex to reduce the fluorescence intensity. Dynamic quenching means that the fluorescence molecule in the excited state collides with the quenching agent to achieve the quenching effect. The quenching effect of both mechanisms is temperature-dependent, with the quenching constant of the former decreasing with increasing temperature and with no change in fluorescence lifetime, while the opposite is true for the latter [40] (Fig. 5, top left). A typical example is the finding that static quenching occurs during the chelation of Fe3+ with I-MoS2 QD and significantly reduces the fluorescence intensity of I-MoS2 QD. The fluorescence lifetimes of Fe3+-treated I-MoS2 QD did not change significantly, consistent with static quenching [11]. In addition, it has been reported that nitrofuran can quench the fluorescence of N-GQDs by means of dynamic quenching. According to the quenching constants at different temperatures, the quenching constant increases significantly when the temperature of the quenching system increases, which meets the criteria for dynamic quenching [41]. The mechanism mainly involves the analyte directly quenching the fluorescence of the nonmetal-doped QD probe to achieve contaminant detection, which is also applicable to the detection of mercury, cadmium, and azithromycin [42–44].

Among the many sensing principles, PET has become a widespread contaminant detection strategy due to its intuitive and clear sensing mechanisms. The nonmetal-doped QDs (electron donor or electron acceptor) undergo electron transfer with the analyte (electron donor or electron acceptor), mainly consisting of reductive PET and oxidative PET. The driving force for the reduction electron transfer is the energy difference between the highest occupied molecular orbital of the nonmetal-doped QD and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of the analyte. The opposite is true for oxidizing electrons [45] (Fig. 5, bottom left). For instance, Fe3+ can quench the blue fluorescence of S-Si QDs via PET. Since Fe3+ is a paramagnetic transition metal ion with half-filled 3d orbitals, it can act as an effective electron acceptor or hole, inducing nonradiative PET. The functional groups on the surface of the S-Si QDs can coordinate with Fe3+ to form a complex, which causes the excited state electrons of the S-Si QDs to transition into the half-filled 3d empty orbitals of Fe3+, resulting in fluorescence quenching [21]. In addition, it has been reported that when Fe3+ binds to the surface of sulfur-doped MoOx quantum dots (S-MoOx QDs), photoinduced excitation may release S-MoOx QD energy in a nonradiative manner. The photoelectrons leading to S-MoOx QDs can be transferred to the gap d orbitals of Fe3+, which ultimately leads to the quenching of S-MoOx QD fluorescence [46]. Other substances such as fluoride ions and ciprofloxacin can also cause fluorescence quenching of nonmetal-doped QD probes by electron transfer [47,48].

IFE is based on the specific spectra of the probe and the analyte to change the fluorescence intensity of the probe to achieve the detection purpose. There are five main conditions for this: (1)sufficient overlap between the direct excitation or emission spectra of the donor and the acceptor; (2)the absorption of the absorber is sensitive and highly selective to changes in analyte concentration; (3) the fluorescent agent is independent of the analyte to ensure that the fluorescent agent acts as a fluorescent indicator in a pure IFE sensing system; (4) the absorption and fluorescence features of the selected absorber and fluorescer should be free from the influence of external factors, such temperature, pH, etc; (5) the fluorescence should not be quenched by the absorber, so it is better that the absorber/fluorescer pair exists in the charge repulsion condition [49] (Fig. 5, lower right). Such as the excitation curves of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) overlap well with the absorption spectra of copper nanoclusters, but the addition of dinotefuran led to the aggregation of copper nanoclusters and thus reduced the absorption of copper nanoclusters by excitation radiation, resulting in weaker IFE on S-CQDs. This process reduces the IFE between S-CQDs and copper nanoclusters, leading to the recovery of S-CQD fluorescence and the realization of proportional fluorescence sensing by dinotefuran [50]. Due to the specificity of this mechanism, nonmetal-doped QDs mainly bind to metal nanoclusters or fluorescent molecules to achieve fluorescence “turn-off” and colorimetric fluorescence sensing. Therefore, they are advantageous in the detection of nitrite, tetracycline, and phosphate [26,51,52].

FRET has remarkable potential for application in contaminant detection due to its special energy transfer mechanism. FRET is a typical process in which the absorption of light stimulates the donor molecule’s lowest unoccupied molecular orbital. Followed by the simultaneous excitation of the donor, which returns to a state of equilibrium by transferring energy to the acceptor molecule. A necessary condition for this system is that the donor’s emission spectrum must overlap with the acceptor’s absorption spectrum, their distance is generally less than 10 nm, and the fluorescence lifetime of nonmetal-doped QD probes changes [53] (Fig. 5, top right). An overlap in fluorescence excitation spectra and emission spectra between N-MXene QDs and the acceptor o-phenylenediamine was reported. The fluorescence color appeared bright yellow, and the average fluorescence lifetime decreased to 3.62 ns after Cu2+ addition, confirming the FRET-induced fluorescence quenching process. With d-penicillamine present, the fluorescence intensity of N-MXene QDs at 450 nm was significantly enhanced due to the synergistic result of Cu2+ and D-PA, which is an important strategy to implement the “turn-off-on” detection mechanism [54]. In addition, this sensing mechanism also plays an important role in the detection of picric acid and carbendazim [55,56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of nonmetal doped QD probes in contaminant detection
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, QD probes based on nonmetallic element doping have been verified to occupy high stability, excellent optical properties, and environmental friendliness. They are widely used in the detection of heavy metals, harmful non-metallic ions, pesticides, and antibiotics. Table 2 summarizes the applications of nonmetal-doped QD probes for the detection of these contaminants in recent years. In the next section, we describe the practical applications of fluorescence sensing based on nonmetal-doped QD probes for the detection of these contaminants.

Heavy metals such as Hg, Cd, Fe, and Cu can be released into water, soil, and the atmosphere, posing a threat to water resources and plant growth. Therefore, detecting heavy metals is crucial to safeguard freshwater resources and promote proper plant growth and development. Nonmetal-doped QD probes can detect these heavy metals. For example, N, B-codoped CQDs can detect Hg2+ in wastewater, and their limit of detections (LODs) reach 5.3 nM and 7.8 nM in fluorescence and colorimetric modes, respectively. There was a positive correlation in the linear range of 0 − 0.2 μM in fluorescence mode and 0 − 0.3 μM in colorimetric mode [57]. N-GQDs enable efficient detection of Cd (II) in wastewater. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs was found to linearly increase with Cd (II) concentrations in the range of 1.0 − 25.0 μg/L (LOD is about 1.09 μg/L) [58]. S-doped CDs can accurately detect the presence of As3+ in water, and the LOD for As3+ is as low as 32 pM [59]. In addition, it has been reported that N-MoS2 QDs can be used as a fluorescent probe for Fe3+ and exhibit fluorescence recovery in the presence of ascorbic acid. The fluorescence “turn-off” detection of Fe3+ in water and ascorbic acid in serum was realized, with LODs reaching 1 μM and 960 nM, respectively [60]. The blue fluorescent sensor N-MQDs is capable of detecting Cu2+ and Fe3+ in water samples. The N-MQDs exhibited excellent linearity and sensitivity for the detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions over a concentration range of 0.5 − 500 μM. The LODs for the Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions were calculated to be 0.17 and 0.15 μM, respectively. Compared to previous N-MXene QDs, N-MQDs have a lower LOD for Cu2+ ions [61]. Below, we take Hg2+ as an example to describe the application of nonmetal-doped QD probes for heavy metal detection in detail.

Hg2+ pollution is an important factor threatening aquatic organisms, and thus, achieving its detection is of great significance for the protection of freshwater aquatic species. Sulfur, oxygen-codoped carbon nitride quantum dot (SCNQD) based Hg2+ fluorescent sensors with excellent fluorescence properties were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis using thiourea and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt dihydrate as raw materials [62]. The sensor has high selectivity and sensitivity to Hg2+. Hg2+ had the most pronounced effect on SCNQD fluorescence quenching in solutions with different heavy metals present, while other metals had no significant effect on PL. The sensitivity of SCNQDs against Hg2+ follows a linear relationship in the concentration range from 0.01 to 1 μM. The lowest LOD is 0.01 nM, which is lower than the World Health Organization standard limit of 10 nM. In addition, when metal ion solutions were dropped onto SCNQD-loaded filter paper and observed under 365 nm illumination, the fluorescence of SCNQD was significantly quenched by Hg2+ ( Fig. 6a). Filter papers loaded with SCNQDs can be used for preliminary detection of Hg2+ at sites or in a laboratory, and this filter paper-based detection system for Hg2+ is cost-effective and can play a vital role in water quality monitoring for public health [62].

Harmful nonmetallic ions can combine with water to form acids, leading to acidification of water bodies and soils, which is an important factor threatening the survival of water ecosystems and soil microorganisms. Effective detection of these ions is essential for the protection of water ecosystems and the safety of beneficial microorganisms. Nonmetal-doped QD probes are an important method for detecting nonmetal ions. For example, Br-doped CDs fluorescent sensing can achieve accurate detection of phosphate in actual water samples. It has a linear response over the 0.4 − 22 μM phosphate concentration range with a LOD of 0.25 μM and can be used for confocal imaging of phosphate in HeLa cells [52]. By forming the N, F-codoped CDs@Cu2+ sensing platform and based on the high affinity of S2- to Cu2+, the N, F-codoped CDs @Cu2+ showed a linear response to S2- in industrial wastewater in the concentration range of 0 − 200 μM with a LOD of 347 nM [63]. In addition, green fluorescent probes of N, P-MoOx QDs can be applied for sensitive detection of the concentrations of permanganate in water. The linear range for MnO4 - concentration was 0.08 − 25 μM with the LOD of 10 nM, and the linear range for captopril concentration was 0.1 − 31 μM with LOD of 15 nM [33]. We next describe the application of nonmetal-doped QD probes for the detection of hazardous nonmetal ions using NO2 - as an example.

NO2 - is commonly found in drinking water and processed foods, and its presence increases the risk of cancer. Timely detection of NO2 - is crucial for protecting water resources and human health. Nonmetal-doped QD probes have emerged as an important way of monitoring NO2 -. For example, boron, nitrogen-codoped carbon dots (B, N-CDs) fluorescent probes were prepared by hydrothermal method using citric acid, boric acid, and branched polyethyleneimine to enable fluorescent sensing of NO2 - [64]. In the presence of NO2 -, the PL intensity decreased gradually with increasing NO2 - concentration. Then, for the first time, B, N-CDs were doped into a polyvinyl alcohol film that has excellent optical properties for accurate detection of NO2 - in real meat samples. A perfect linear relationship between the normalized signal and the analyte concentration was achieved, and a smartphone program was used to detect NO2 - in meat in real-time. The solution was able to detect NO2 - in meat in situ with high sensitivity over a linear range from 20 to 50 mM [64] (Fig. 6b). The scheme uses CDs prepared by B, N codoping with excellent sensing properties, and the application smart sensor developed using a smartphone is able to realize the NO2 - content in real samples [64]. Therefore, the combination of sensing of nonmetal-doped QD probes and smart software is an important tool to improve real-time monitoring and can effectively enhance its application in real samples.

The heavy use of pesticides can contaminate soil, water, and air and persist in soil and water, resulting in a reduction in biodiversity and environmental pollution. Achieving effective detection of pesticides is crucial to safeguard biodiversity and freshwater safety. A series of nonmetal-doped QD probes were developed to rapidly detect pesticide residues in the environment, such as S-doped GQDs, which can detect cafuran and thiram in water and apples. The green fluorescent S-doped GQDs in the aqueous phase exhibit a LOD of 0.45 ppb for cafuran and 1.6 ppb for thiram [65]. The detection of glyphosate by fluorescence “off-on” of N-Si QDs is reported. N-Si QDs are able to quench fluorescence by interacting with Cu2+ and recover fluorescence when glyphosate is added. The model showed that N-Si QDs fluorescence was linearly related to glyphosate concentration in the range from 0.1 to 1 μg/mL, with a LOD as low as 7.8 ng/mL [66]. A fluorescent probe based on N-CDs has also been reported for the detection of atrazine residues in water. Interestingly, the probe was designed to increase the fluorescence intensity by hydrogen bonding between atrazine and -NH2 on the surface of N-CDs to achieve sensitive detection with a LOD of 33 pM [67]. Here, we only focus on how nonmetal-doped QDs were used to detect neonicotinoid pesticides accurately.

The massive use of neonicotinoid pesticides is an important factor causing pesticide residues on crops, and achieving effective detection is very important to protect food and environmental safety. Using the excellent optical properties of nonmetallic QDs in combination with composite materials is an important tool for detecting neonicotinoid pesticides. For example, porous multiporous imprinted microspheres (MIMs) fluorescent sensors of N-CDs were prepared using a one-pot reverse microemulsion surface imprinting method, which enabled accurate detection of flonicamid [68]. N-CD@MIMs can form specific recognition sites for flonicamid due to the functionalization of 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane on the surface and can enable the -NH3 group to form hydrogen bonds with flonicamid, which in turn quenches the fluorescence of N-CD@MIMs. Subsequent photosensitization in real vegetable samples and water showed that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@MIMs was effectively quenched by flonicamid at very low concentrations. The fluorescence quenching correlation with the concentration of flonicamid showed good linearity in the range of 0.02 − 0.2 μg/g with LOD of 0.0059 μg/g, and the recovery of 79.3 %− 99.2 % was achieved [68] ( Fig. 7a). It has been confirmed that the novel composite material consisting of nonmetal-doped QD probes and other polymeric materials is successful and has great potential for utilization in the detection of pesticide residues [68].

Misuse of antibiotics, such as improper disposal of medical waste, discharge of industrial effluents, and overuse of antibiotics in farming, can lead to water pollution and soil contamination. Their accurate detection is of great value to protect aquatic organisms and beneficial microorganisms. The development of nonmetal-doped QD probes provides a good solution for the detection of antibiotics. For example, N, B, and P-codoped CDs were able to effectively detect the presence of tetracyclines in wastewater by fluorescence sensing and colorimetric sensing, with a LOD of 1.73 nM in fluorescence sensing mode and 0.46 μM in colorimetric sensing mode [69]. N, S-codoped GQDs were reported to detect furazolidone in wastewater, and N, S-codoped GQD fluorescence was linearly quenched in the concentration range of 8.0 − 250.0 μg/L, with a LOD of 2.1 μg/L [70]. In addition, the direct use of N, S-codoped CQDs as fluorescent nanosensors for the sensitive fluorescence determination of salinomycin and maduramicin was reported for the first time [71]. The fluorescence quenching of the N, S-codoped CQDs was broadly linear with the concentration of each drug in the range of 10.0 − 300.0 μM, and the LODs of salinomycin and maduramicin were 2.07 μM and 1.34 μM, respectively. It had good detection ability in some different matrices, such as feed, beef, and chicken [71]. Therefore, we take tetracyclines and some other antibiotics as examples to specify the application of nonmetal-doped QD probes for antibiotic detection.

The misuse of large amounts of tetracyclines, alkane-modified fluoroquinolones (AFQs), and oxazine-modified fluoroquinolones (OFQs) can cause water pollution and affect the activity of soil microorganisms. Their effective detection is of great value in protecting freshwater resources and the safety of beneficial microorganisms. Sensing with nonmetal-doped QD probes provides an important solution to achieve efficient detection. A novel fluorescent probe of multi-doped graphene quantum dots (M-GQDs) containing fluorine, nitrogen, and phosphorus atoms is reported for the differentiation and monitoring of tetracyclines, AFQs, and OFQs [72]. Specifically, the fluorescence of M-GQDs is quenched by tetracyclines due to the occurrence of IFE. The introduction of AFQs results in fluorescence enhancement with a redshift. Meanwhile, the electron-accepting properties of the oxazine ring in OFQs make two emission peaks exist at 419.0 nm and 498.0 nm, resulting in a color change from blue to cyan (Fig. 7b). Here, doxycycline, pefloxacin, and levofloxacin are chosen as the typical detection models for tetracyclines, AFQs, and OFQs, respectively. The quantitative evaluation is established, covering 5.00 − 50.00 µM for doxycycline, covering 3.00 − 200.00 µM, and 3.00 − 30.00 µM for pefloxacin and levofloxacin with LODs of 39.30 nM, 48.60 nM, and 35.10 nM, respectively [72]. Based on the high accuracy and multisample detection of M-GQDs, a 3D microfluidic paper-based analytical device sensing platform is proposed for the simultaneous detection and differentiation of tetracyclines, AFQs, and OFQs in real samples such as tap water, milk, and meat. The results of smartphone analysis showed that the recoveries of the targets in the samples ranged from 97.30 % to 105.3 % [72]. Thus, the strategy not only opens new avenues for utilizing M-GQDs in sensing applications but also presents broad application prospects for real-time on-site sensing in smart devices.

In this work, we primarily focus on the synthesis methods, optical properties of nonmetal-doped QD probes, sensing mechanisms, and applications in contaminant detection. Nonmetal-doped QDs are widely used for the detection of environmental pollutants due to their unique fluorescence sensing properties. However, large-scale applications still have some challenges: (1) obtaining high-performance probes: How to optimize the preparation route to ensure that the interaction and distribution of dopant atoms or molecules with the QDs are as expected, and thus obtaining highly photostable and lifetime doped non-metallic QDs materials. (2) selectivity and sensitivity: Fluorescence sensing probes need to have high selectivity and sensitivity to detect target molecules and distinguish other interfering substances. For nonmetal-doped QDs, it is a challenge to select the appropriate dopant atoms or molecules for specific recognition. (3) real-time monitoring: Fluorescence sensing probes are often required to monitor the dynamic processes of target molecules in real-time. For nonmetal-doped QDs, real-time monitoring with high temporal and spatial resolution is a challenge. In addition, the signal stability and background interference of probes in complex biological environments need to be addressed.

A summary of nonmetal-doped QDs reveals their importance as fluorescent probes for the detection of environmental contaminants. More types of nonmetal-doped QD probes may be developed in the future to expand their applications and improve detection efficiency. Meanwhile, the combination of nonmetal-doped QD probes with other nanomaterials and smartphones is a developing trend to realizing the real-time monitoring of pollutants. In conclusion, the development of nonmetal-doped QD probes for fluorescence sensing is expected to design environmentally friendly, highly reliable, convenient, and fast detection tools. With the further development of technology and increasing demand for applications, more research and innovations will drive the development in this field.
